summary: Policy-backed control for Windows sensors. Microsoft documents the same device policy path and value name that the app writes, and the policy description matches the disabled behavior.

setting.name: Windows Sensors
setting.casual_explanation: Windows can use hardware sensors such as orientation, ambient light, motion, or other environmental sensors. This policy decides whether sensors are allowed.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable sensors when they want a stricter privacy baseline or when a device should not expose sensor-driven behavior.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Disabling sensors reduces access to sensor data, but tablets, convertibles, brightness adjustments, presence-aware experiences, and other sensor-based features can stop working properly.
setting.professional_notes: Sensors.admx defines both user and machine variants of DisableSensors. The current app writes the machine policy at HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LocationAndSensors\DisableSensors with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows does not force the machine policy for sensors.

allowed_values.label: Sensors allowed
allowed_values.meaning: The policy is disabled and sensors remain available.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes DisableSensors = 1 under the machine policy key, which matches the documented disable behavior.

windows_defaults.label: Windows device-policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 7 and newer systems with Location and Sensors policy support

states.rationale: The machine policy is not configured by default.

windows_defaults.notes: Sensors.admx contains both user and machine mappings. This record validates the machine-side mapping used by the current app.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Devices that rely on sensor-aware behavior
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want ordinary Windows sensor behavior to remain available.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want to cut off sensor access across the device.
tradeoffs: Sensor-driven apps and system experiences can keep working.
No hard restriction is enforced for sensor access.

recommended_profiles.label: Privacy focused
intended_for: Managed devices that should not expose sensor data
avoid_for: Tablets, convertibles, or devices that depend on sensors
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to block Windows sensor access at the device-policy level.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You rely on orientation, brightness, presence, or other sensor-based features.
tradeoffs: Sensor data access is blocked by policy.
Sensor-dependent hardware and software behavior can degrade or stop working.

evidence.title: Microsoft Policy CSP: DisableSensors
evidence.summary: Official documentation for the policy that controls whether Windows sensors are allowed.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft Sensors.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file shows DisableSensors for both user and machine scope with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0 under the LocationAndSensors policy key.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes DisableSensors = 1 under the machine policy key for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Existing tweak provenance record
evidence.summary: The repo already tracks provenance for this tweak in the current catalog output.

evidence.title: nohuto win-config mirror - DisableSensors policy
evidence.summary: Sensors.admx shows DisableSensors under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LocationAndSensors. The mirrored ExplainText says enabling the policy turns off the sensor feature and prevents all programs from using sensors, while disabling or not configuring it leaves sensors available. EnabledValue=1 and DisabledValue=0 are explicit.

decision.why: Microsoft documents DisableSensors directly in the ADMX_Sensors Policy CSP and maps it to Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LocationAndSensors\DisableSensors. The same page describes that enabling the policy disables all Windows sensor features, and the app writes that exact machine policy surface.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: If you enable this policy setting, all Windows Sensor features are disabled. ADMX mapping: Name DisableSensors ... Registry Key Name Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\LocationAndSensors ... Registry Value Name DisableSensors.
validation_proof.notes: The ADMX_Sensors Policy CSP page explicitly names the exact machine policy path and value name the app writes, and its policy description matches the disabled behavior. Added nohuto mirror corroboration via nohuto-disablesensors-admx.
